units.name: Definition, motivation, and core applications
units.description: Introduces what multimodality means, why it matters, and the main task families that motivate multimodal modeling.
units.summary: Kiela begins by noting that “multimodality” is somewhat ill-defined and uses it operationally as combining different information types, especially text and images in this lecture [ts=121s][ts=156s]. He gives three motivations: human understanding is intrinsically multimodal, internet data is naturally multimodal, and scaling language models may require adding non-text data rather than only more text [ts=175s][ts=212s]. The McGurk-effect discussion is used as an intuition pump that perception itself depends on cross-modal interaction, and he then lays out the main application categories: retrieval, image captioning, text-to-image generation, visual question answering, multimodal classification, and richer downstream understanding/generation [ts=255s][ts=301s][ts=325s].

key_points.text: In this lecture, a modality is treated as a type of information source such as text, image, speech, audio, or smell.
Multimodality matters because human perception is multimodal, web data is multimodal, and future scaling may require adding non-text data.
The McGurk effect illustrates that changing visual mouth motion can change perceived speech even when audio stays the same.
Core multimodal tasks include retrieval, captioning, text-to-image generation, VQA, and multimodal classification.

units.name: Early multimodal modeling patterns
units.description: Surveys pre-transformer deep multimodal ideas built around shared embedding spaces, grounding, captioning, attention, and text-conditioned generation.
units.summary: The early recipe is to encode vision and language separately and align them in a shared embedding space with a similarity score and margin-based objective so similar image-text items move together and dissimilar ones move apart [ts=476s][ts=491s]. Kiela then walks through bag-of-visual-words style grounding, CNN-plus-word-embedding multimodal word vectors, and skip-gram-style prediction of image features from words, arguing that grounding gives richer semantic representations [ts=598s][ts=680s]. He extends the story from words to sentences, where LSTM or recursive sentence encoders are aligned with images, and notes that sentence encoders trained to predict pictures transfer well to downstream NLP tasks [ts=720s][ts=780s]. The segment ends with encoder-decoder captioning, visual attention that aligns generated words to image regions, and text-conditioned GAN image generation as the early path toward modern text-to-image systems [ts=813s][ts=873s][ts=904s].

key_points.text: A basic early multimodal model aligns vision and language encoders in one embedding space using a similarity score and margin loss.
Bag of visual words uses SIFT keypoints, local descriptors, clustering, and counts to form a visual analogue of bag of words.
Predicting image features for words or sentences can yield richer grounded semantic representations.
Image captioning emerges by replacing the source-language encoder in seq2seq with a ConvNet image encoder.
Attention lets a captioning model align generated words like “stop” to the relevant image region such as a stop sign.
GANs enable text-conditioned image generation by training a generator to fool a discriminator while conditioning on text prompts.

units.name: Features and multimodal fusion
units.description: Explains how images are featurized and how modalities can be combined through early, middle, and late fusion.
units.summary: Kiela first notes practical headaches in multimodal learning: one modality can dominate, extra modalities can add noise, coverage may be missing, and model design becomes harder [ts=985s][ts=1008s]. He then reviews image featurization options: region features from object detectors, generic ConvNet features, and Vision Transformer patch embeddings, contrasting them with the more familiar tensorized representation of text [ts=1085s][ts=1149s][ts=1210s]. The core concept is multimodal fusion: combining vectors can be as simple as inner products or linear projections, or more expressive with elementwise multiplication, gating, attention, and bilinear variants [ts=1238s][ts=1285s]. He formalizes the design space as early, middle, and late fusion, and uses FiLM as an example where question representations generate gamma/beta modulation parameters that condition each ResNet block [ts=1302s][ts=1354s].

key_points.text: A major multimodal failure mode is modality dominance, where text can be strong enough that a model learns to ignore the image.
Region features come from running an object detector first and encoding each detected sub-image with a ConvNet.
A Vision Transformer treats image patches as tokens and feeds them through a standard transformer architecture.
Fusion can be early, middle, or late depending on whether modalities interact from the start, after separate processing, or only at the score level.
FiLM conditions a vision network by applying learned multiplicative gamma and additive beta modulation at each ResNet layer.

units.name: From CLIP to multimodal transformers and FLAVA
units.description: Covers contrastive vision-language learning, BERT-era multimodal transformers, and the move toward unified multimodal foundation models.
units.summary: Late fusion is reframed as modern contrastive learning, with CLIP as the canonical example: independently encode text and images, use in-batch negatives, and rank matched pairs above mismatched ones [ts=1402s][ts=1441s]. Kiela emphasizes that CLIP’s leap came less from a novel loss than from transformer encoders and massive web-scale paired data, enabling prompt-based zero-shot classification and robust transfer; ALIGN and LAION continue the same scaling story with more data [ts=1503s][ts=1561s][ts=1667s]. He then walks through BERT-era multimodal transformers such as VisualBERT, ViLBERT, MMBT, PixelBERT, UNITER, and ViLT, contrasting single-stream versus dual-stream fusion and showing the trend from detector features toward raw patch-based transformer inputs [ts=1828s][ts=1869s][ts=2021s][ts=2061s]. The segment culminates in FLAVA, a “one model to rule them all” design that jointly pretrains image-only, text-only, and multimodal objectives, combining masked image modeling, masked language modeling, image-text matching, and a global contrastive loss in one shared foundation model [ts=2207s][ts=2331s][ts=2399s].

key_points.text: CLIP trains text and image encoders independently and uses an in-batch contrastive ranking objective over aligned image-text pairs.
Prompting with templates like “a photo of a ...” enables zero-shot image classification with CLIP-style models.
Scaling data and compute, as in ALIGN and LAION, improves contrastive vision-language models even when the core idea stays the same.
VisualBERT is a single-stream model that concatenates visual and textual inputs into one transformer, while ViLBERT uses two streams with cross-attention.
ViLT removes detector-style preprocessing and feeds image patches directly into the transformer.
When controlled for data and training setup, many multimodal BERT variants are nearly equivalent, implying data matters more than minor architectural tweaks.
FLAVA jointly trains unimodal and multimodal objectives so one model can handle vision, language, and their combination.

units.name: Generative multimodal foundation models and training practice
units.description: Explains the shift to generative multimodal systems, frozen-language-model adapters, Flamingo-style architectures, and related practical trade-offs.
units.summary: The lecture next shifts from contrastive/discriminative models to generative ones such as SimVLM and CoCa, arguing that caption generation and decoder-based objectives can yield richer representations than pure matching losses [ts=2441s][ts=2455s]. Kiela then describes the frozen-language-model recipe: keep a strong LM fixed, learn projections into its token space, and exploit in-context learning behavior for multimodal grounding [ts=2486s][ts=2528s]. Flamingo is presented as a key example: a frozen Chinchilla LM is paired with a vision encoder, perceiver resampler, and gated cross-attention so multiple images can condition generation, while BLIP-2 shows a similar projection strategy with OPT or FlanT5 [ts=2563s][ts=2640s][ts=2756s]. The unit closes with practical Q&A points: frozen layers preserve pretrained abilities, earlier fusion usually yields richer understanding if affordable, and unified models like FLAVA are motivated partly by robustness to missing modalities [ts=2944s][ts=3009s][ts=3256s].

key_points.text: Generative multimodal models such as SimVLM and CoCa add decoders and caption-generation objectives to learn richer representations.
A strong recipe is to freeze the language model and learn a projection from visual features into the language-model token space.
Few-shot multimodal grounding can emerge when a frozen LM receives projected visual inputs plus in-context examples.
Flamingo uses a vision encoder, perceiver resampler, and gated cross-attention before a frozen Chinchilla language model.
BLIP-2 learns a mapping between an image encoder and large language models such as OPT or FlanT5.
Multimodal chain-of-thought prompting improves reasoning benchmarks like ScienceQA by asking the model to generate a rationale first.
Freezing means gradients may flow through a module, but its weights are not updated so pretrained abilities are preserved.
Earlier fusion is usually better for richer multimodal understanding, while late fusion is cheaper and easier to train.

units.name: Evaluation and benchmark design
units.description: Argues that multimodal progress depends on benchmark design that prevents shortcut learning and isolates true multimodal reasoning.
units.summary: Kiela frames evaluation as a central scientific problem: the point is not just building larger models, but checking whether they are actually good at multimodal reasoning [ts=3296s][ts=3330s]. He uses COCO as the canonical richly annotated benchmark for captioning and retrieval, but warns that popular tasks like VQA can be badly shortcut-prone; in an early version, questions like “how many” could often be answered by always predicting 2, without looking at the image [ts=3396s][ts=3458s][ts=3485s]. To show how benchmark construction can force genuine multimodal reasoning, he discusses Hateful Memes, where swapping just the text or the background changes the label, and Winoground, where models must distinguish paired captions such as “plants surrounding a light bulb” versus “a light bulb surrounding plants” [ts=3600s][ts=3741s][ts=3892s]. The broader lesson is that many state-of-the-art systems still fail on compositional or distribution-tail cases, and even strong generators reflect skew in their training data rather than robust world understanding [ts=4014s][ts=4088s].

key_points.text: Multimodal evaluation should measure whether a system truly reasons across modalities rather than exploiting dataset shortcuts.
COCO is a richly annotated benchmark supporting captioning, retrieval, segmentation, and other vision-language tasks.
A flawed VQA benchmark let models ignore the image because many “how many” questions were answered correctly by predicting 2.
Hateful Memes was designed so changing one modality can flip the label, forcing genuine multimodal reasoning.
Winoground tests compositionality by pairing captions with the same words in different orders and requiring the correct visual match.
Some state-of-the-art models perform below random chance on Winoground-style composition tests.
Strong generative models still reveal training-distribution biases, such as preferring spoons over forks and struggling with counting.

units.name: Beyond images: audio, video, embodied settings, 3D, smell, and future trends
units.description: Extends the lecture beyond images and closes with forecasts about multimodal foundation models.
units.summary: The final section broadens the lecture beyond image-text pairs to speech, audio, video, embodied environments, 3D generation, and even smell [ts=4134s][ts=4464s]. Whisper is highlighted as a large-scale speech model trained on 680,000 hours of multilingual transcribed audio using log-mel spectrogram inputs into a transformer, and Kiela notes that audio can often be treated as a vision-like representation via spectrograms [ts=4185s][ts=4204s][ts=4245s]. For video, he argues that many existing image-text recipes transfer with frame subsampling or keyframe selection, and mentions trimodal systems such as MERLOT Reserve that add audio on top of video-and-language modeling [ts=4352s][ts=4380s]. He closes with more speculative directions—simulated environments for grounded learning, text-to-3D generation, olfactory embeddings, multimodal scaling laws, retrieval augmentation, and the idea that future foundation models will integrate many modalities while still needing much better evaluation [ts=4495s][ts=4587s][ts=4665s][ts=4679s].

key_points.text: Whisper trains on 680,000 hours of multilingual multitask speech data using log-mel spectrograms and a transformer.
Audio can sometimes be reduced to a spectrogram and processed with standard vision architectures to obtain strong auditory features.
Video models often subsample frames or keyframes and then reuse familiar vision-language transformer recipes.
MERLOT Reserve is an example of a trimodal model that combines video, language, and audio.
Simulated environments may be important because humans ground language by acting in and observing the world.
Text conditioning can extend beyond images to 3D generation such as point clouds or CAD-like design.
Olfactory embeddings can be built from chemical-compound statistics and may correlate with human similarity judgments better than pure word vectors for smell-grounded concepts.
Future directions include one general multimodal foundation model, multimodal scaling laws, retrieval augmentation, and much better evaluation.
